hero.title: Welcome to the World of Pandas
hero.subtitle: BambooBay is your interactive portal about Giant Pandas, Red Pandas and global conservation. Discover facts, calculate with us, and make a difference.

features.title: What Awaits You
features.subtitle: From facts to active support - everything in one place.

encyclopedia.title: Panda Encyclopedia
encyclopedia.description: Dive into the world of Giant Pandas and Red Pandas. Facts about habitat, diet, and behaviour.

calculator.title: Bamboo Calculator
calculator.description: How much bamboo would you have to eat if you were a panda? Our calculator makes it tangible.

conservation.title: Conservation Projects
conservation.description: Learn about the threats pandas face and how you can actively contribute to their protection.

interactive.title: Interactive & Modern
interactive.description: Multilingual, accessible and optimised for all devices - education without barriers.

fact1.title: Black and White
fact1.text: The Giant Panda's striking coloration is its most recognisable feature - and likely serves as camouflage.

fact2.title: No Hibernation
fact2.text: Unlike other bears, Giant Pandas do not hibernate, as bamboo is available year-round.

fact3.title: False Thumb
fact3.text: Pandas have an enlarged wrist bone that functions like a thumb, helping them grip bamboo stalks.

fact4.title: Rare Births
fact4.text: Female pandas are only fertile for a few days each year, making breeding extremely challenging.

fact5.title: Red Panda
fact5.text: The Red Panda is not closely related to the Giant Panda but belongs to its own family, Ailuridae.

fact6.title: Bamboo Specialists
fact6.text: Pandas spend up to 14 hours a day eating bamboo, as it contains very little nutritional value.

encyclopedia.title: Panda Encyclopedia
encyclopedia.subtitle: Everything you need to know about pandas - compact and well-researched.
encyclopedia.noResults: No entries found. Try a different search term.

greatPanda.title: Giant Panda
greatPanda.description: The Giant Panda is one of the world's most recognisable animals and serves as a symbol of global conservation. It lives in the mountain forests of central China at elevations of 1,500 to 3,000 metres. Its black-and-white colouring and gentle nature have made it a beloved animal worldwide. Despite its bear-like body, its diet is almost entirely plant-based.

redPanda.title: Red Panda
redPanda.habitat: Temperate forests of the Himalayas and southwest China
redPanda.description: The Red Panda, despite its name, is not closely related to the Giant Panda. It is a slender, cat-like animal with a bushy, ringed tail and reddish-brown fur. It lives in the cool mountain forests of the Himalayas and southwest China. As a nocturnal and crepuscular animal, it spends much of its time in trees. Its population is declining sharply.

bambooBelt.title: The Bamboo Belt
bambooBelt.description: The bamboo belt of central China is the primary habitat of the Giant Panda. These mountain regions between 1,200 and 3,400 metres are characterised by dense bamboo thickets. Deforestation and the expansion of human settlements have fragmented this habitat. China has established protected areas to preserve the remaining forest areas.

bambooNutrition.title: Bamboo as Food
bambooNutrition.description: Bamboo is the main food source for the Giant Panda. Since bamboo is very low in nutrients, an adult panda must eat 12 to 38 kilograms of it daily. It spends up to 14 hours a day eating. The panda has evolutionarily adapted to this diet, although its digestive system is still that of a carnivore.

qinlingPanda.title: Qinling Panda
qinlingPanda.description: The Qinling Panda is a subspecies of the Giant Panda and differs in its dark brown-and-white colouring instead of black-and-white. It lives exclusively in the Qinling Mountains in Shaanxi Province. The population comprises only about 200-300 individuals. This subspecies was officially recognised only in 2005 and is one of the rarest bears in the world.

habitatFragmentation.title: Habitat Fragmentation
habitatFragmentation.description: One of the greatest problems for pandas is the fragmentation of their habitat by roads, settlements and agriculture. Isolated panda populations can no longer move freely or exchange individuals for reproduction. China is therefore actively building wildlife corridors that connect isolated forest patches, enabling genetic exchange.

calculator.title: Bamboo Calculator
calculator.subtitle: What if you were a panda? Find out how much bamboo you would need to eat each day.
calculator.explanationText: A Giant Panda eats about 20-40% of its body weight in bamboo every day. Since bamboo contains very few calories, a panda must consume enormous amounts. For your weight of {{weight}} kg, that would mean: you would need to eat {{bamboo}} kg of bamboo daily to meet your energy needs.
calculator.explanationRedPanda: A Red Panda eats about 20-30% of its body weight each day. Since it is much smaller, the absolute amount remains small. For your weight, that would be {{bamboo}} kg of bamboo daily.
calculator.funFact1: That would be roughly {{loaves}} loaves of bread - every day!
calculator.funFact2: A panda spends up to 14 hours a day eating to achieve this.
calculator.bambooStalks: That corresponds to about {{stalks}} bamboo stalks.
calculator.sliderHint: Move the slider to adjust your weight
calculator.comparisonText: An adult Giant Panda weighs 75-135 kg and eats 12-38 kg of bamboo per day.
calculator.howItWorksText: We calculate your bamboo needs based on the physiology of the respective panda species. Since humans have different digestive efficiency, this is naturally a thought experiment - but an insightful one!

conservation.title: Conservation Projects
conservation.subtitle: Understanding threats, taking action, and taking responsibility - for the pandas of tomorrow.

threats.title: Threats
threats.subtitle: What endangers pandas and their habitat?

habitat.title: Habitat Loss
habitat.description: Deforestation, agriculture and urbanisation destroy and fragment the bamboo rainforests that pandas need to survive.

poaching.title: Poaching
poaching.description: Although rare today, poaching remains a threat. Pandas are hunted for their fur and for the illegal trade.

climate.title: Climate Change
climate.description: Rising temperatures are shifting bamboo distribution, which threatens the long-term food supply for pandas.

isolation.title: Genetic Isolation
isolation.description: Fragmented populations can no longer interbreed freely, threatening the genetic diversity and resilience of the species.

measures.title: Conservation Measures
measures.subtitle: What is being done - and what can you do?

reserves.title: Nature Reserves
reserves.description: China operates over 67 nature reserves that together protect a large portion of panda habitat.

corridors.title: Wildlife Corridors
corridors.description: Connecting strips between isolated forest patches enable pandas to move and exchange genetically.

breeding.title: Breeding Programmes
breeding.description: Breeding centres like the Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding research and improve captive reproduction.

education.title: Education and Awareness
education.description: Local communities and schoolchildren are educated about conservation to build long-term support.

projects.title: Fictitious Sponsorships and Donations
projects.subtitle: Support one of our conservation projects - symbolic, but with great impact.
projects.disclaimer: Note: These projects are fictitious and serve illustration purposes only. For real donations, we recommend the WWF or the Chengdu Panda Base Fund.

project1.title: Minshan Corridor
project1.description: Restoration and protection of a 50 km wildlife corridor between two isolated panda populations in the Minshan Mountains.

project2.title: Red Panda Patrol
project2.description: Training local rangers to protect the Red Panda and its forest habitats in the eastern Himalayas.

project3.title: Bamboo Garden Wolong
project3.description: Reforestation of 200 hectares of degraded bamboo forest in the Wolong Nature Reserve as a direct food source for wild pandas.

project4.title: Education Initiative Chengdu
project4.description: Conservation education in 30 primary schools around the panda belt, inspiring the next generation to protect wildlife.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: Protecting your personal data is important to us. This privacy policy informs you about how we handle your data.

section1.title: 1. Controller
section1.text: The controller for data processing on this website is Leroy [Surname], reachable at contact@bamboobay.ch. This website is operated as a static site on GitHub Pages.

section2.title: 2. Collection and Storage of Data
section2.text: This website does not actively collect personal data. No cookies are set, no tracking tools are used, and no user data is passed to third parties. Your language preference is stored exclusively locally in your browser (localStorage) and does not leave your device.

section3.title: 3. Hosting
section3.text: This website is hosted via GitHub Pages. GitHub may create server logs as part of its hosting service, which contain technical access data (IP address, timestamp, accessed page). This data is collected and processed by GitHub. For more information, see GitHub's privacy policy.

section4.title: 4. External Fonts
section4.text: This website loads fonts from Google Fonts. This establishes a connection to Google's servers, which may result in the transmission of your IP address. If you have concerns, we recommend using an appropriate browser blocker.

section5.title: 5. Your Rights
section5.text: You have the right to access, correction, deletion and restriction of processing of your data, to the extent that it is collected. For questions, contact contact@bamboobay.ch.

section6.title: 6. Changes to This Policy
section6.text: We reserve the right to update this privacy policy as necessary. The current version is available on this page. Last updated: January 2025.

disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
disclaimer.text: The content of this website has been carefully researched and compiled. However, we accept no liability for the accuracy, completeness and timeliness of the content. The fictitious donation amounts and projects on the Conservation Projects page serve illustration purposes only.
